result:
I started writing because words gave me a room I could step into and arrange any way I pleased, and no-one could evict me from it. I was four when my parents realized that failure came as naturally to me as breathing; I skipped crawling and went straight to catastrophic toddler errors—toppling head-first off the changing table, gargling mouthfuls of aquarium water, threading our goldfish through the VCR slot in the sincere hope that the tape would give it color. What looked like clumsiness was actually an early form of experimentation: I wanted to know what fell and what floated, what fit and what didn’t. Decades later, I sit on the fire escape of the apartment my salary can only barely cover, knees wedged between rust and brick, and I’m still running that experiment. The variables have merely changed. Now I test ideas, relationships, ambitions, and the strange configuration of bones that make up an almost-40-year-old human body. Gravity has definitely become more opinionated.

It is March, always March whether the calendar says so or not. The winds are audible advice everyone is too polite to take. Someone three floors down plays what must be a 1990s mixtape in its entirety, fake-dolby hiss intact: “No Woman No Cry” bleeds into “Creep (Acoustic)” bleeds into an Alanis Morissette song that taught teenage girls how to scream in key. I think: memory is less a vault than a thrift store. Half the items are chipped, most are mislabeled, none contain their original context, yet we buy them anyway because only they recognize the weird shape of our hunger. So goes nostalgia.

Inside the window I just climbed out of, the heating is broken. Outside, the radiators in another apartment produce rattles that sound like typewriter ghosts composing ransom notes for twenty-first-century lethargy. Between these two temperatures exists my budget winter: coat indoors, hat and scarf to shower and sleep. I once believed adulthood would arrive with matching bowls, financial literacy, and a recipe for lasagna that never gave me heartburn. I now own four spoons, three of which are the kind that come with yogurts. That counts, right?

Last week the pharmacy cashier carded me for cold medicine and said, “You have the skin of an immortal.” The compliment felt like a receipt that folds itself into a paper swan. Later, in the mirror, I noticed that the line between laughing and frowning at myself has narrowed to a hair; I can tilt my face one degree and change the genre of my expression from comedy to tragedy without altering the script. The skin age-miracle is, I suspect, a trick of poor fluorescent light at the drugstore. Still, I pocketed the myth: maybe I am a very slow witch who forgot her spells. Maybe the world will allow me enough time to remember.

In grammar school I was the chubby kid tasked with erasing chalkboards because my arms were wide enough to sweep maximum surface area in one swipe. It felt powerful, dethroning each letter—making ideas vanish felt like producing them. Mrs. Tovani, a teacher shaped like a cinnamon stick and scented like chopped onions, once caught me humming while scrubbing and said, “If you enjoy annihilation this much, you should become a writer.” I had no clue what that entailed but assumed it was a career path where sweat-scented people got to destroy entire sentences with the ceremonial flick of a rubber rectangle. Sign me up, Mrs. Onion. A destiny was sealed under the talc ghost of linear equations.

It turns out writing is less about erasing and more about moving grief around on the page until it resembles focus. I write because the alternative is a mouth full of cotton and a head full of dust devils. I write because otherwise the grocery list metamorphoses into six stanzas and my boss wonders why olive oil rhymes with absolute. On the worst days the poem I have not yet written stands at the foot of the bed like a debt collector with a flashlight for a mouth: You promised metaphors. You borrowed prepositions. You owe.

Despite all this I still can’t resist romanticizing words. Language is the only lover who never asks me to choose between honesty and orgasm; she takes every syllable like a consecrated wafer and rolls her eyes when I apologize afterward for the crumbs. She forgives redundancy yet secretly marks it in red ink, the kind that disappears only under moonlight, so every full moon I feel her edit flaring across my sleep. Sometimes I wake with indentation of a semicolon on my cheek.

They say the city hums but this one sings soprano at 3 a.m. Just now an ambulance passes—note held long enough to split along its harmonic spine. A neighbor screams back at it in the key of leave-me-alone. Somewhere near the Williamsburg Bridge an Uber driver leans on his horn the way cathedral boys tease the organ after mass, just to hear the cavern answer. I conduct from the fire escape, casting shadow batons up at the purple-bruise sky. I insist that all this noise is longing wearing decibel as disguise. A city’s desire has no mute button, only remix.

Often I fantasize about compiling an atlas that charts peripheral love. Page one: map of all the passengers in 1973 who pressed the buzzer a second too early because the hand on their shoulder felt too much like belonging. Page seven: territories where mothers believed a lullaby could double as apology for the war outside. The centerfold is a fold-out heart the size of a burlesque stage spotted wherever strangers served coffee with stirred-in empathy. The legend will warn: scale distorted by nostalgia, boundaries redrawn nightly.

Once, when I was ten, I lost my mother in a department store. It was only forty-five minutes, but the store had eleven floors and a penthouse cafeteria, which inflation of architecture made it feel mythic. I kept ascending escalators, each one shortening my breath and lengthening the plot. Between mannequins wearing mustard trench coats and discount racks of last year’s prom dresses, I rehearsed how to survive once she was gone forever. I considered living under the handbag display where florescent light was buttery. I planned to eat samples of cologne for protein. I resolved to learn the mannequins’ alabaster stillness so sales associates would overlook me. By the time security located me I had become a temporary orphan, and the reunion was a comedic reversal: Mom weeping into denim, me apologizing for accumulating urgent, epic grief so quickly. She bought me a chocolate éclair to seal the injury and I inked the sugary custard into memory as proof of love’s sugary ransom.

I’ve longed suspected that Déjà vu is the brain doing a quick metadata search and the soul realizing it crash-landed in someone else’s draft. That sensation—that the moment has been memorized backward—maybe is the universe gently proving reincarnation contains continuity errors. Maybe the first draft had a different squirrel crossing the street, an alternate brand of toothpaste. But the tone stayed identical. Minor edits, major déjà.

In college, a professor with bifocals pinched onto his nose like a clothespin proposed we write our autobiographies backwards, ending with conception. I tried it and discovered my beginning was murkier than any climax. Worse: I ruined potential sequels by giving away how I died in the prologue. He gave me a B for courage and an A for giving him hiccups of inspiration; we both knew grading expired the moment ink dried.

I keep a private list titled “proof I exist when no one looks.” Entries vary: the ghost breath circle on a metro window in February; the specific crack my knees make climbing stairs; hummingbird mid-air pause before the feeder. Items 28-34 are kids’ sidewalk chalk drawings that spell my initials accidental, the geographical coincidence so blatant it feels like code from a younger self across town. Item 47 is forgetting to close the fridge overnight and only the avocados noticing. Last week I added #113: the automatic door at Walgreens recognizing my heartbeat because surely sensors now record the microwave pulse under my ribs, data sold to an insurance firm. Yet still, somewhere in an algorithmic sieve, a number registers my liveness. There.

On Tuesdays the laundromat hosts a woman who irons poems onto pillowcases with a press so hot the fabric carries scorch memory long after threads fray. She does it gratis, claims raw language improves REM sleep. I always leave my pillowcase plain; I’m afraid of dreams that arrive with footnotes. Once she attempted to coax me by reciting a stanza about moonlight as orphan glue. I told her my tenancy already leaks enough landlordless light. She blinked twice, iron hovering like an interrogation, then lowered it and said, “Shame. I’d hoped tonight your skull might rehearse the echo before the original ache.”

My mother WhatsApps me photos of her succulents instead of photos of herself since the stroke. Each cactus filtered to dim saturation, caption like “Rosalind grew a centimeter!” My replies scroll heart emojis, but my chest folds at the implication: Mom has outsourced her biography to plants. I research the language of succulents: signs when overwatered (leaves wrinkle) under watered (leaves wrinkle). The paradox is my mother’s signature. She stays alive by drinking just enough loss to keep blooming inside the lack. I imagine one day texting back: Mom, water me.

A hundred or so winters prior I would have died already. Pneumonia at thirty-one, childbirth at twenty-two, maybe stoning at nineteen because my imagination refused male threading. Now specters of bygone futures hover kindly. They form a parliament of what-if selves, each bearing the wardrobe of their decade: the tubercular poet clutching lace hanky, the frontier schoolmarm with chalkdust on gloves, the disco activist navigating neon and rage. They vote on my behalf: the motion passes to continue breathing by nail-biting margin.

The cost of not dying is ridiculously high. My student loans have accrued grandchildren. Every bill contains a secret poem titled “compound interest,” internal rhyme that tightens like handcuffs. The gig economy dresses itself with adjectives (flexible, boundaryless) and devours afternoons the way snakes inhale eggs. I have taught workshops titled “how to monetize authenticity,” which even as I write it looks like a cry for help tattooed in snake-oil ink. Yet some student always asks: but what if my MFA bruises are too ripe for LinkedIn? I’ve started answering: collect bruises until you can curate them into wallpaper. Interior décor is destiny.

Last winter the radiator produced such banging I dreamed the dead used pipes as telegraph. The code formed the phrase “warmth is divisible,” which I interpreted to mean love can be quantum, split between lovers without halving. The next morning I scribbled this theory onto Post-it notes strewn across neighbors’ mailboxes—anonymous hope folded into utility. By noon someone added beneath: “but cold is absolute.” The handwriting was cramped, almost pleading. I imagined them shirtless by a window, skin like like unopened mail. We never met, yet we collaborated on a physics of emotional thermodynamics.

Subway logic: any delay is explained if you anthropomorphize the train as anxious. If you miss the 7:03, perhaps it is because the G line sent it a long paragraph text the night prior—unfinished argument, heart-emoji and boundary dispute. We riders are merely audience to this drama of machines. Announcements apologize in metallic baritone, but that is press release. Truth is R142 model W4 inherited grandma’s guilt.

I’ve fallen in love seven times; all but the present one constitute discarded drafts. Former loves now file themselves under continents: one in Buenos Aires teaching tango to the recently widowed, one in a Portland potting shed rewriting her DNA to fit heirloom seed names. My favorite phase is the ghosting month, how silence can feel like origami made of air. You keep unfolding and unfolding until you realize the message was emptiness precisely cut.

The current beloved sleeps beside me now, headphones still in, podcast mumbling about black holes. I adore the way drool pools on the pillow in homage to understated vulnerability. We gather each other like fireflies, glow, then puncture our palms to share bioluminescence. This metaphor is messy, but love is mostly stains.

Sometimes I eat grief like popcorn—light, colonizing every crevice—then pretend kernels lodged in gums are nuance. A therapist with hoop earrings once suggested grief needs a landing strip. So far all I’ve built is trampoline. My feelings boomerang. My mother texts me another succulent. The cycle centrifuges.

Memory smells like Sharpie on overhead projector transparencies: chemically forbidden. I recall Mrs. Tovani writing with green marker the word FORESHADOW on a sheet. The plastic warped in the lamp’s heat, causing the word to shiver like prophecy. At age eight I resolved to become a sorcerer of spoilers. They called it narrative instead of magic but paydays arrived indistinguishable.

Weather is the city’s therapist: cloudy today, New York lies on couch; tomorrow rain, and we all bawl cathartically; heatwave: collective hyperventilation. We invoice climate for co-pay, but storm systems file bankruptcy.

I developed synesthesia briefly while feverish. Every chord tasted like rice paste, and apologies were ultraviolet. In that hallucination I drafted an entire novel by belching. When I recovered, words regained their stoic crust. The loss felt like forgetting lyrics just as the choir begins.

I have seen through three presidential elections the same Jimmy Johns guy who shouts “Free smells!” with escalating existential irony. Last Tuesday he added, “If you don’t like the nation, invent a smell for it.” I ordered extra onions as contribution.

Yesterday I walked past the shuttered theater where I first kissed someone legally. The marquee still advertises a 2019 musical about climate change performed by teenagers in sequinned hazmat suits. Half the bulbs were dead spelling “ANTE THE F TE M L.” In the vacancy I tasted popcorn shaped like 1999: butter and fear.

Each age you reinvent euphemism for penis. Childhood: “peepee box,” adolescence: “the disappointing wand,” adulthood: a mutual fund noise. Language grows up ashamed of itself.

I read banned books the way teenagers pierce cartilage: both vigorously and for the same reason—adults fucking lied.

Whenever I doubt my body, I recall mitochondria conducting cities inside me, and suddenly citizens revolt against negative self-talk.

My best friend from college produces artisanal divorce papers: marbled paper, gold-leaf heartbreak. The couples frame them. A weird shrine to unsalvageable.

Night at 4:03 a.m. feels like sneaking into the axis mundi’s maintenance closet. You find spare purpose jumbled with loose hourglass sand. You pocket half an ounce, spill another.

I’ve decided insomnia is a polyglot trying to conjugate the verb “to rest.” Syntax folds like lawn chairs.

Occasionally I tweet private jokes to my unborn children. The account has zero followers and 67 password-protected tweets. Example: “Defrosted ice cube poet; do not date before seasoning.” The future owes me laughs.

Once I measured love by how gently someone corrected my name pronunciation. Now I measure by how quickly they recognize I’m grieving the alphabet itself.

At twenty-nine I dyed my hair glacier to threaten climate deniers. Results: mostly compliments from stylists who charged extra for habitat destruction metaphors.

I still write letters to the Ouija planchette that scared me at sleepovers. Addressed: “Dear Wandering Yes.” I never stamp them; hope transcends bad zip code.

I have used the word “meander” 112 times in my diaries, proving lack of direction is my cardinal trait.

The dictionary entry for ‘still’ should contain a footnote: especially potent at 4 a.m. in between breath and next catastrophe. Still. Still? Still.

In dreams my inner critic looks like the waiter who once spilled soup on my lap apologizing profusely while refilling the bowl. He whispers: hot take.

I collect fortune cookie fortunes literally stick them on white wall until that geography of improbable futures resembles a sky.

I envy GPS voice—aware of flaws yet calmly recalculating. I keep ‘recalculating’ as mantra but pronounce it with dramatic sigh.

I believe laughter depreciates. Each recycled joke loses 3% currency until only nostalgia holds value. Invest early.

If consciousness is browser tab, mine has 567 open; frozen auto-play, burning laptop thighs of soul.

Last fall city council debated whether to name a street after a poet who died choking on vowels. Compromise: alleyway.

In the darkest hour, the neighbor’s Wi-Fi titled “It Hurts When IP” keeps my phone awake in solidarity.

I once kissed a stranger on Halloween because my mask featured “borrowed joy.” He tasted like licorice and tomorrow.

I cherish email exchanges with old professors. They sign off “semper grammaticus,” I respond “ever dangling,” weeping emoji.

Every scar writes the rough draft of survival. My knee marks punctuation from meteorological playground collision: comma pause.

Errors in my credit report read like autocorrect memoir. They swore I earned a living “as a street illusionist frog,” which sounds better than actual.

My printer jams only when printing lease reminders. Paper rebels against bureaucracy.

Once I tracked moon phases by how often strangers said “take care.” Waxing equals amplified tenderness.

To-do lists should come with trigger warnings for desire.

I store compassion in tea tins. When opened, Darjeeling apologizes for empire.

Some evenings I mute audiobook narrator mid-sentence to hear author breathe.

I love that the word ‘listen’ anagrams to ‘silent,’ and both are instructions vital.

Whenever I remember mother saving foil for reuse, I fold my anxieties into cranes, wings sharp enough to butter air.

Memory is sieve second cousin to imagination.

Yesterday a child pointed at my cane and asked whether I surf internet sidewalk. I answered: crest breaks at fifth avenue.

Retinas behold billboard: YOU ARE ENOUGH (fine print: terms apply). I squint until serif shrinks.

I dreamt I translated the sound of subway doors into lullaby. Infant city slept without derailment.

Sometimes I sign emails “your humble fragment.” No one corrects; perhaps destiny autocorrects.

Once I feared time was heroin: once tasted, always chasing. Then I met someone who sips minutes like wine. Now I chasten myself to be a sommelier.

I once wrote a poem that only contained punctuation. It had pauses so powerful it made readers sob the unsaid.

In every group chat exists a friend we have killfiled emotionally but never muted, like a museum fire alarm wired to nostalgia.

I like thunderstorms, they give faults reverb.

Whenever the elevator is stuck I pretend to be an astronaut docking. “Houston, we have sequins.”

My therapist taught me grounding: name five objects you see. I name: ghost, ghost, ghost, ghost, coffee mug. The room smells of honesty oxidizing.

The suicide prevention hotline volunteer answered with voice so warm I pictured pie steam. I imagine her gifting strangers mental slice.

I write this at 2:47 a.m. The vent exhales like my body downloading sigh update 3.2.

My horoscope warns: avoid emotional brinkmanship. I reply: what if brink is only edge of pie?

I believe each generation elects one song to collective scream-sing until death parts chorus. Our playlist ends in corporate VPN ping.

Often I mourn friendships I ghosted before maturity; I send postcards without return address, message reads: “I licked this stamp for you.”

Memory of loss sometimes opens like elevator shaft. I fall ten floors into yesterday’s grocery store crying among the eggplants.

I keep phone on airplane mode to imagine I’m pilot of thoughts; no turbulence, only suspension.

I find solace in subtitles because they caption lives I mumble.

I applaud myself with blink if I make it one more sunrise without bankrupting wonder.

I’ve drafted novels on receipts; plot arcs curve like thermal paper. Fades eventually; so does relevance.

There is a café shaped like expectation, serves croissants the size of apologies. Butter drips like forgiveness.

In text to friend I confessed: “I am falling behind.” Autocorrect changed to: “I am falling behind music.” Felt truer melancholy.

I record laughter of broken Nespresso machine, add delay effect, sell as meditation app noise.

I suspect endorphins are tiny cheerleaders with collagen lips yelling “gimme a ‘sero’! gimme a ‘tonin’!”

Every ceiling fan wobble is just dancer insecure.

I believe sky rents its blue hue from Instagram hashtag pool. Debt accumulates at sunset.

Yesterday I gave five dollars to man whose sign read “need hugs.” We hugged; his cologne was cedar resilience.

On dating apps I write bio: “seeking co-author for footnotes of eventual biography.” I get superliked by librarian. Date forthcoming. Assuming footnotes flirt.

I save voicemails from dead people inside stuffed bear. His paw smelled like lithium battery last I checked.

I diagnosed myself with reverse claustrophobia: fear of spaces too open for memory walls.

My refrigerator greets me when opened. Light sighs: “again?”

Petty joy: using payphone to call cell from past as prank.

Tuesday I renamed nervous system to chord progression; G major finally serves serotonin.

Sometimes I wanna fold city into paper fortune teller and pick “you still got hope” always.

I baptize unread emails “maybe later souls.” Congregation multiplies.

Every subway poem I scrawl has same last line: “still love you despite commute.”

I once wore jacket backwards entire day to avoid the future boring into neckline. It worked halfway; got coffee.

Memory’s USB drive corrupted so I podcast hiccups instead.

Whenever anxiety builds tower in throat I demolish with exhale graffitied ‘not quits’.

I practice answering “how are you” honestly once monthly, like quarterly taxes for soul.

I wish there existed perfume scent of horizon after rainfall. Titled: aftermath ambition.

My fingerprint feels too unique some days, longs crowd membership.

I contemplate finishing this manifesto but that implies closure.

Instead I end mid-breath, ellipsis floating like undone shoelace

intentionally inviting trip or momentum.

May we all tangle forward.

The current beloved sleeps beside me now, headphones still in, podcast mumbling about black holes. I adore the way drool pools on the pillow in homage to understated vulnerability. We gather each other like fireflies, glow, then puncture our palms to share bioluminescence. This metaphor is messy, but love is mostly stains.

Sometimes I eat grief like popcorn—light, colonizing every crevice—then pretend kernels lodged in gums are nuance. A therapist with hoop earrings once suggested grief needs a landing strip. So far all I’ve built is trampoline. My feelings boomerang. My mother texts me another succulent. The cycle centrifuges.

Memory smells like Sharpie on overhead projector transparencies: chemically forbidden. I recall Mrs. Tovani writing with green marker the word FORESHADOW on a sheet. The plastic warped in the lamp’s heat, causing the word to shiver like prophecy. At age eight I resolved to become a sorcerer of spoilers. They called it narrative instead of magic but paydays arrived indistinguishable.

Weather is the city’s therapist: cloudy today, New York lies on couch; tomorrow rain, and we all bawl cathartically; heatwave: collective hyperventilation. We invoice climate for co-pay, but storm systems file bankruptcy.

I developed synesthesia briefly while feverish. Every chord tasted like rice paste, and apologies were ultraviolet. In that hallucination I drafted an entire novel by belching. When I recovered, words regained their stoic crust. The loss felt like forgetting lyrics just as the choir begins.

I have seen through three presidential elections the same Jimmy Johns guy who shouts “Free smells!” with escalating existential irony. Last Tuesday he added, “If you don’t like the nation, invent a smell for it.” I ordered extra onions as contribution.

Yesterday I walked past the shuttered theater where I first kissed someone legally. The marquee still advertises a 2019 musical about climate change performed by teenagers in sequinned hazmat suits. Half the bulbs were dead spelling “ANTE THE F TE M L.” In the vacancy I tasted popcorn shaped like 1999: butter and fear.

Each age you reinvent euphemism for penis. Childhood: “peepee box,” adolescence: “the disappointing wand,” adulthood: a mutual fund noise. Language grows up ashamed of itself.

I read banned books the way teenagers pierce cartilage: both vigorously and for the same reason—adults fucking lied.

Whenever I doubt my body, I recall mitochondria conducting cities inside me, and suddenly citizens revolt against negative self-talk.

My best friend from college produces artisanal divorce papers: marbled paper, gold-leaf heartbreak. The couples frame them. A weird shrine to unsalvageable.

Night at 4:03 a.m. feels like sneaking into the axis mundi’s maintenance closet. You find spare purpose jumbled with loose hourglass sand. You pocket half an ounce, spill another.

I’ve decided insomnia is a polyglot trying to conjugate the verb “to rest.” Syntax folds like lawn chairs.

Occasionally I tweet private jokes to my unborn children. The account has zero followers and 67 password-protected tweets. Example: “Defrosted ice cube poet; do not date before seasoning.” The future owes me laughs.

Once I measured love by how gently someone corrected my name pronunciation. Now I measure by how quickly they recognize I’m grieving the alphabet itself.

At twenty-nine I dyed my hair glacier to threaten climate deniers. Results: mostly compliments from stylists who charged extra for habitat destruction metaphors.

I still write letters to the Ouija planchette that scared me at sleepovers. Addressed: “Dear Wandering Yes.” I never stamp them; hope transcends bad zip code.

I have used the word “meander” 112 times in my diaries, proving lack of direction is my cardinal trait.

The dictionary entry for ‘still’ should contain a footnote: especially potent at 4 a.m. in between breath and next catastrophe. Still. Still? Still.

In dreams my inner critic looks like the waiter who once spilled soup on my lap apologizing profusely while refilling the bowl. He whispers: hot take.

I collect fortune cookie fortunes literally stick them on white wall until that geography of improbable futures resembles a sky.

I envy GPS voice—aware of flaws yet calmly recalculating. I keep ‘recalculating’ as mantra but pronounce it with dramatic sigh.

I believe laughter depreciates. Each recycled joke loses 3% currency until only nostalgia holds value. Invest early.

If consciousness is browser tab, mine has 567 open; frozen auto-play, burning laptop thighs of soul.

Last fall city council debated whether to name a street after a poet who died choking on vowels. Compromise: alleyway.

In the darkest hour, the neighbor’s Wi-Fi titled “It Hurts When IP” keeps my phone awake in solidarity.

I once kissed a stranger on Halloween because my mask featured “borrowed joy.” He tasted like licorice and tomorrow.

I cherish email exchanges with old professors. They sign off “semper grammaticus,” I respond “ever dangling,” weeping emoji.

Every scar writes the rough draft of survival. My knee marks punctuation from meteorological playground collision: comma pause.

Errors in my credit report read like autocorrect memoir. They swore I earned a living “as a street illusionist frog,” which sounds better than actual.

My printer jams only when printing lease reminders. Paper rebels against bureaucracy.

Once I tracked moon phases by how often strangers said “take care.” Waxing equals amplified tenderness.

To-do lists should come with trigger warnings for desire.

I store compassion in tea tins. When opened, Darjeeling apologizes for empire.

Some evenings I mute audiobook narrator mid-sentence to hear author breathe.

I love that the word ‘listen’ anagrams to ‘silent,’ and both are instructions vital.

Whenever I remember mother saving foil for reuse, I fold my anxieties into cranes, wings sharp enough to butter air.

Memory is sieve second cousin to imagination.

Yesterday a child pointed at my cane and asked whether I surf internet sidewalk. I answered: crest breaks at fifth avenue.

Retinas behold billboard: YOU ARE ENOUGH (fine print: terms apply). I squint until serif shrinks.

I dreamt I translated the sound of subway doors into lullaby. Infant city slept without derailment.

Sometimes I sign emails “your humble fragment.” No one corrects; perhaps destiny autocorrects.

Once I feared time was heroin: once tasted, always chasing. Then I met someone who sips minutes like wine. Now I chasten myself to be a sommelier.

I once wrote a poem that only contained punctuation. It had pauses so powerful it made readers sob the unsaid.

In every group chat exists a friend we have killfiled emotionally but never muted, like a museum fire alarm wired to nostalgia.

I like thunderstorms, they give faults reverb.

Whenever the elevator is stuck I pretend to be an astronaut docking. “Houston, we have sequins.”

My therapist taught me grounding: name five objects you see. I name: ghost, ghost, ghost, ghost, coffee mug. The room smells of honesty oxidizing.

The suicide prevention hotline volunteer answered with voice so warm I pictured pie steam. I imagine her gifting strangers mental slice.

I write this at 2:47 a.m. The vent exhales like my body downloading sigh update 3.2.

My horoscope warns: avoid emotional brinkmanship. I reply: what if brink is only edge of pie?

I believe each generation elects one song to collective scream-sing until death parts chorus. Our playlist ends in corporate VPN ping.

Often I mourn friendships I ghosted before maturity; I send postcards without return address, message reads: “I licked this stamp for you.”

Memory of loss sometimes opens like elevator shaft. I fall ten floors into yesterday’s grocery store crying among the eggplants.

I keep phone on airplane mode to imagine I’m pilot of thoughts; no turbulence, only suspension.

I find solace in subtitles because they caption lives I mumble.

I applaud myself with blink if I make it one more sunrise without bankrupting wonder.

I’ve drafted novels on receipts; plot arcs curve like thermal paper. Fades eventually; so does relevance.

There is a café shaped like expectation, serves croissants the size of apologies. Butter drips like forgiveness.

In text to friend I confessed: “I am falling behind.” Autocorrect changed to: “I am falling behind music.” Felt truer melancholy.

I record laughter of broken Nespresso machine, add delay effect, sell as meditation app noise.

I suspect endorphins are tiny cheerleaders with collagen lips yelling “gimme a ‘sero’! gimme a ‘tonin’!”

Every ceiling fan wobble is just dancer insecure.

I believe sky rents its blue hue from Instagram hashtag pool. Debt accumulates at sunset.

Yesterday I gave five dollars to man whose sign read “need hugs.” We hugged; his cologne was cedar resilience.

On dating apps I write bio: “seeking co-author for footnotes of eventual biography.” I get superliked by librarian. Date forthcoming. Assuming footnotes flirt.

I save voicemails from dead people inside stuffed bear. His paw smelled like lithium battery last I checked.

I diagnosed myself with reverse claustrophobia: fear of spaces too open for memory walls.

My refrigerator greets me when opened. Light sighs: “again?”

Petty joy: using payphone to call cell from past as prank.

Tuesday I renamed nervous system to chord progression; G major finally serves serotonin.

Sometimes I wanna fold city into paper fortune teller and pick “you still got hope” always.

I baptize unread emails “maybe later souls.” Congregation multiplies.

Every subway poem I scrawl has same last line: “still love you despite commute.”

I once wore jacket backwards entire day to avoid the future boring into neckline. It worked halfway; got coffee.

Memory’s USB drive corrupted so I podcast hiccups instead.

Whenever anxiety builds tower in throat I demolish with exhale graffitied ‘not quits’.

I practice answering “how are you” honestly once monthly, like quarterly taxes for soul.

I wish there existed perfume scent of horizon after rainfall. Titled: aftermath ambition.

My fingerprint feels too unique some days, longs crowd membership.

I contemplate finishing this manifesto but that implies closure.

Instead I end mid-breath, ellipsis floating like undone shoelace

intentionally inviting trip or momentum.

May we all tangle forward.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
